draft.introduction: Clement of Alexandria’s Protrepticus (c. 195 CE) is an exhortation to the Greeks that attempts to lead educated pagans toward Christianity through philosophical persuasion. Clement treats Greek philosophy as a preparation for the Gospel rather than mere error.

translation.text: O truly sacred mysteries! O pure light! I am led by torchlight to the vision of heaven and of God. I am made holy by initiation. The Lord is the hierophant who reveals the sacred. He marks the initiate with his seal and illuminates the one who believes.
translation.original_text: Ὦ τῶν ὁσίων ὡς ἀληθῶς μυστηρίων· ὦ φωτὸς ἀκηράτου· δάιδουχοῦμαι τοὺς οὐρανοὺς καὶ τὸν θεὸν ἐποπτεῦσαι

translator_notes.note: Clement deliberately borrows the language of the mystery religions—initiation, torchlight, hierophant, seal—and applies it to Christian baptism and illumination. This is not mere rhetoric but a theological claim: Christianity is the true mystery of which Eleusis and the Bacchic rites were shadows.
